abstract: In recent years, the demand for 3D content has grown exponentially with the intelligent upgrade of interactive media, extended reality (XR), and Metaverse industries. In order to overcome the limitations of traditional manual modeling approaches, such as labor-intensive workflows and prolonged production cycles, revolutionary advances have been achieved through the convergence of novel 3D representation paradigms and artificial intelligence generative technologies. In this survey, we conduct a systematic review of the cutting-edge achievements in static 3D object and scene generation, as well as establish a comprehensive technical framework through systematic categorization. We start our analysis with mainstream 3D object representations. Subsequently, we delve into the technical pathways of 3D object generation based on four mainstream deep generative models: Variational Autoencoders, Generative Adversarial Networks, Autoregressive Models, and Diffusion Models. Regarding scene generation, we focus on three dominant paradigms: layout-guided generation, lifting based on 2D priors, and rule-driven modeling. Finally, we critically examine persistent challenges in 3D generation and propose potential research directions for future investigation. This survey aims to provide readers with a structured understanding of state-of-the-art 3D generation technologies while inspiring researchers to undertake more exploration in this domain.
title: Recent Advances in 3D Object and Scene Generation: A Survey

abstract: Recent advances in diffusion models have revolutionized video generation, offering superior temporal consistency and visual quality compared to traditional generative adversarial networks-based approaches. While this emerging field shows tremendous promise in applications, it faces significant challenges in motion consistency, computational efficiency, and ethical considerations. This survey provides a comprehensive review of diffusion-based video generation, examining its evolution, technical foundations, and practical applications. We present a systematic taxonomy of current methodologies, analyze architectural innovations and optimization strategies, and investigate applications across low-level vision tasks such as denoising and super-resolution. Additionally, we explore the synergies between diffusionbased video generation and related domains, including video representation learning, question answering, and retrieval. Compared to the existing surveys (Lei et al., 2024a;b; Melnik et al., 2024; Cao et al., 2023; Xing et al., 2024c) which focus on specific aspects of video generation, such as human video synthesis (Lei et al., 2024a) or long-form content generation (Lei et al., 2024b), our work provides a broader, more updated, and more fine-grained perspective on diffusion-based approaches with a special section for evaluation metrics, industry solutions, and training engineering techniques in video generation. This survey serves as a foundational resource for researchers and practitioners working at the intersection of diffusion models and video generation, providing insights into both the theoretical frameworks and practical implementations that drive this rapidly evolving field. A structured list of related works involved in this survey is also available on this https URL.
title: Survey of Video Diffusion Models: Foundations, Implementations, and Applications

abstract:
Interactive Generative Video (IGV) has emerged as a crucial technology in
response to the growing demand for high-quality, interactive video content
across various domains. In this paper, we define IGV as a technology that
combines generative capabilities to produce diverse high-quality video content
with interactive features that enable user engagement through control signals
and responsive feedback. We survey the current landscape of IGV applications,
focusing on three major domains: 1) gaming, where IGV enables infinite
exploration in virtual worlds; 2) embodied AI, where IGV serves as a
physics-aware environment synthesizer for training agents in multimodal
interaction with dynamically evolving scenes; and 3) autonomous driving, where
IGV provides closed-loop simulation capabilities for safety-critical testing
and validation. To guide future development, we propose a comprehensive
framework that decomposes an ideal IGV system into five essential modules:
Generation, Control, Memory, Dynamics, and Intelligence. Furthermore, we
systematically analyze the technical challenges and future directions in
realizing each component for an ideal IGV system, such as achieving real-time
generation, enabling open-domain control, maintaining long-term coherence,
simulating accurate physics, and integrating causal reasoning. We believe that
this systematic analysis will facilitate future research and development in the
field of IGV, ultimately advancing the technology toward more sophisticated and
practical applications.

keyInsights: IGV systems require integration of generation, control, memory, dynamics and intelligence capabilities
Different application domains have unique requirements but share common technical challenges
Real-time generation and open-domain control remain significant bottlenecks
Long-term coherence and physics simulation accuracy are critical for practical applications
originalProblem: Lack of unified framework for understanding and developing interactive generative video systems
Growing need to make AI-generated videos interactive and controllable for practical applications
Limited understanding of technical challenges across different IGV application domains
results: Established formal definition and taxonomy for Interactive Generative Video
Identified key technical challenges including real-time generation, open-domain control, and causal reasoning
Provided structured roadmap for future IGV research and development
Demonstrated potential applications across gaming, robotics and autonomous vehicle testing
solution: Proposed comprehensive five-module framework for IGV systems
Systematic analysis of IGV applications across gaming, embodied AI, and autonomous driving
Detailed examination of technical challenges and future directions for each module
paper_summary.summary: A comprehensive survey establishes the framework and taxonomy for Interactive Generative Video (IGV) systems across gaming, embodied AI, and autonomous driving applications, decomposing the technology into five essential modules (Generation, Control, Memory, Dynamics, and Intelligence) while analyzing technical challenges and opportunities in each component.

title: A Survey of Interactive Generative Video

abstract: With the rapid development of AI-generated content (AIGC), video generation has emerged as one of its most dynamic and impactful subfields. In particular, the advancement of video generation foundation models has led to growing demand for controllable video generation methods that can more accurately reflect user intent. Most existing foundation models are designed for text-to-video generation, where text prompts alone are often insufficient to express complex, multi-modal, and fine-grained user requirements. This limitation makes it challenging for users to generate videos with precise control using current models. To address this issue, recent research has explored the integration of additional non-textual conditions, such as camera motion, depth maps, and human pose, to extend pretrained video generation models and enable more controllable video synthesis. These approaches aim to enhance the flexibility and practical applicability of AIGC-driven video generation systems. In this survey, we provide a systematic review of controllable video generation, covering both theoretical foundations and recent advances in the field. We begin by introducing the key concepts and commonly used open-source video generation models. We then focus on control mechanisms in video diffusion models, analyzing how different types of conditions can be incorporated into the denoising process to guide generation. Finally, we categorize existing methods based on the types of control signals they leverage, including single-condition generation, multi-condition generation, and universal controllable generation. For a complete list of the literature on controllable video generation reviewed, please visit our curated repository at this https URL.

organization_info.name: The Hong Kong University of Science and Technology (Guangzhou)

keyInsights: Diffusion Models (UNet-based and Diffusion Transformers) and Autoregressive models are the predominant generative paradigms for high-quality, temporally coherent video generation.
Specialized modules like ControlNet, adapters, and motion modules are crucial for injecting and managing various non-textual control signals within foundational models.

The integration of multiple control conditions (universal control) presents complex challenges in balancing signals and maintaining consistency, yet is essential for fulfilling intricate user intents.
originalProblem: Existing video generation models, primarily text-to-video, lack precise control over complex content beyond simple text prompts.
Users struggle to express intricate, multi-modal, and fine-grained intents for generated video outputs, limiting practical applicability.
A systematic and comprehensive review specifically focusing on the integration of various non-textual control signals for video synthesis was missing in the literature.
results: Systematically categorized and reviewed 246 papers across seven control modalities: structure, ID, image, temporal, audio, other, and universal control.

Identified a trend towards Diffusion Transformers (DiT) and autoregressive models for improved scalability and long-range temporal coherence, moving beyond earlier GAN/VAE and UNet-based diffusion approaches.

Highlighted persistent challenges such as achieving robust temporal consistency, preserving identity across frames, ensuring physical plausibility, and effectively integrating conflicting multi-modal controls, while also proposing key future research directions.
solution: Conducted a systematic literature review of papers from 2020-2025 using PRISMA guidelines, identifying 246 relevant works.
Developed a multi-layered taxonomy to classify controllable video generation methods based on six dimensions, primarily categorizing them by seven types of control modalities.
Analyzed how diverse non-textual conditions (e.g., pose, depth, identity, audio, camera motion) are incorporated into generative models to guide video synthesis.
paper_summary.summary: A systematic survey provides a comprehensive overview of controllable video generation, categorizing methods by diverse input control signals, examining foundational models, and discussing real-world applications. It identifies key challenges and outlines future research directions, from unified multi-condition orchestration to hybrid autoregressive methods.

title: Controllable Video Generation: A Survey

abstract:
3D scene generation seeks to synthesize spatially structured, semantically
meaningful, and photorealistic environments for applications such as immersive
media, robotics, autonomous driving, and embodied AI. Early methods based on
procedural rules offered scalability but limited diversity. Recent advances in
deep generative models (e.g., GANs, diffusion models) and 3D representations
(e.g., NeRF, 3D Gaussians) have enabled the learning of real-world scene
distributions, improving fidelity, diversity, and view consistency. Recent
advances like diffusion models bridge 3D scene synthesis and photorealism by
reframing generation as image or video synthesis problems. This survey provides
a systematic overview of state-of-the-art approaches, organizing them into four
paradigms: procedural generation, neural 3D-based generation, image-based
generation, and video-based generation. We analyze their technical foundations,
trade-offs, and representative results, and review commonly used datasets,
evaluation protocols, and downstream applications. We conclude by discussing
key challenges in generation capacity, 3D representation, data and annotations,
and evaluation, and outline promising directions including higher fidelity,
physics-aware and interactive generation, and unified perception-generation
models. This review organizes recent advances in 3D scene generation and
highlights promising directions at the intersection of generative AI, 3D
vision, and embodied intelligence. To track ongoing developments, we maintain
an up-to-date project page:
this https URL

keyInsights: Each generation paradigm offers distinct advantages: procedural for control, neural 3D for geometry, image-based for photorealism, video-based for temporal coherence
Synthetic datasets provide precise annotations but lack diversity, while real-world scans offer realism but limited annotations
Evaluation requires multiple complementary metrics covering fidelity, consistency, controllability, diversity and plausibility
originalProblem: Existing surveys focus on narrow subdomains or treat scene generation peripherally
Lack of systematic coverage of modern 3D representations and generation paradigms
Need for unified understanding of evaluation metrics and datasets across approaches
results: Identified key technical challenges across paradigms including efficiency, controllability, and semantic consistency
Mapped the application landscape across scene editing, robotics, embodied AI, and autonomous driving
Established framework for comparing methods through standardized evaluation protocols
Created maintained GitHub repository tracking ongoing developments in the field
solution: Structured categorization of methods into four major paradigms
Analysis of trade-offs between approaches across multiple dimensions
Comprehensive review of datasets, metrics, and applications
paper_summary.summary: A comprehensive survey from NTU's S-Lab analyzes 3D scene generation methods across four paradigms (procedural, neural 3D-based, image-based, and video-based), covering technical foundations, datasets, evaluation metrics, and applications while maintaining an updated repository of developments in the field.

title: 3D Scene Generation: A Survey

abstract: Video generation models have emerged as high-fidelity models of the physical world, capable of synthesizing high-quality videos capturing fine-grained interactions between agents and their environments conditioned on multi-modal user inputs. Their impressive capabilities address many of the long-standing challenges faced by physics-based simulators, driving broad adoption in many problem domains, e.g., robotics. For example, video models enable photorealistic, physically consistent deformable-body simulation without making prohibitive simplifying assumptions, which is a major bottleneck in physics-based simulation. Moreover, video models can serve as foundation world models that capture the dynamics of the world in a fine-grained and expressive way. They thus overcome the limited expressiveness of language-only abstractions in describing intricate physical interactions. In this survey, we provide a review of video models and their applications as embodied world models in robotics, encompassing cost-effective data generation and action prediction in imitation learning, dynamics and rewards modeling in reinforcement learning, visual planning, and policy evaluation. Further, we highlight important challenges hindering the trustworthy integration of video models in robotics, which include poor instruction following, hallucinations such as violations of physics, and unsafe content generation, in addition to fundamental limitations such as significant data curation, training, and inference costs. We present potential future directions to address these open research challenges to motivate research and ultimately facilitate broader applications, especially in safety-critical settings.

keyInsights: Video generation models offer superior high-fidelity, physically consistent spatiotemporal predictions compared to traditional simulators and language models for embodied world modeling.
These models provide broad utility in robotics, acting as cost-effective data generators, dynamics/reward models, scalable policy evaluators, and visual planners.

Significant challenges persist, including hallucinations, violations of physics, poor instruction following, lack of uncertainty quantification, and high computational costs, which impede reliable real-world deployment.
originalProblem: Physics-based simulators for robotics are often limited by simplifying assumptions, expensive asset curation, and a 'sim-to-real gap' for complex phenomena.
Learned Markovian state-based world models struggle to capture intricate physical interaction processes and fine-grained spatial-temporal dependencies in the real world.
Language-only abstractions (LLMs/VLMs) lack the expressiveness needed to accurately model the spatial and temporal dynamics essential for physical robot-environment interactions.
results: The survey identified four key application areas for video models in robotics: data generation/action prediction in imitation learning, dynamics/rewards modeling in reinforcement learning, scalable policy evaluation, and visual planning.

Ten major research challenges were systematically enumerated, encompassing issues like hallucinations, physics violations, uncertainty quantification, long video generation, and prohibitive computational costs.

Specific future research directions were proposed to address these challenges, including integrating physics priors, improving instruction following, and developing robotics-centric evaluation benchmarks for more trustworthy and efficient robotic systems.
solution: The paper provides a systematic review of generative video models, particularly diffusion and flow-matching models, positioning them as 'embodied world models' for robotics.
It categorizes their applications across imitation learning, reinforcement learning, policy evaluation, and visual planning.
The survey identifies key research challenges hindering their adoption and proposes future directions to address these limitations for trustworthy integration into robotics.
paper_summary.summary: A comprehensive survey analyzes video generation models as embodied world models in robotics, detailing their applications in areas like imitation learning and visual planning, while also identifying critical research challenges such as physical consistency and uncertainty quantification. It concludes by proposing future directions for their trustworthy integration into safety-critical robotic systems.

title: Video Generation Models in Robotics - Applications, Research Challenges, Future Directions

abstract:
Recent advancements in video generation have witnessed significant progress,
especially with the rapid advancement of diffusion models. Despite this, their
deficiencies in physical cognition have gradually received widespread attention
- generated content often violates the fundamental laws of physics, falling
into the dilemma of ''visual realism but physical absurdity". Researchers began
to increasingly recognize the importance of physical fidelity in video
generation and attempted to integrate heuristic physical cognition such as
motion representations and physical knowledge into generative systems to
simulate real-world dynamic scenarios. Considering the lack of a systematic
overview in this field, this survey aims to provide a comprehensive summary of
architecture designs and their applications to fill this gap. Specifically, we
discuss and organize the evolutionary process of physical cognition in video
generation from a cognitive science perspective, while proposing a three-tier
taxonomy: 1) basic schema perception for generation, 2) passive cognition of
physical knowledge for generation, and 3) active cognition for world
simulation, encompassing state-of-the-art methods, classical paradigms, and
benchmarks. Subsequently, we emphasize the inherent key challenges in this
domain and delineate potential pathways for future research, contributing to
advancing the frontiers of discussion in both academia and industry. Through
structured review and interdisciplinary analysis, this survey aims to provide
directional guidance for developing interpretable, controllable, and physically
consistent video generation paradigms, thereby propelling generative models
from the stage of ''visual mimicry'' towards a new phase of ''human-like
physical comprehension''.

organization_info.name: Huazhong University of Science and Technology

keyInsights: A three-tier taxonomy—Basic Schema Perception, Passive Cognition, and Active Cognition—classifies video generation approaches based on their level of physics understanding, paralleling human cognitive development.
Physical phenomena relevant to video generation encompass mechanics, optics, thermal properties, and material properties, providing a comprehensive scope for evaluating model capabilities.
The progression from mimicking visual patterns to actively simulating world dynamics with differentiable physics engines represents a path towards more robust physics cognition in AI.
originalProblem: Modern video generation models often produce visually impressive content that lacks physical plausibility, such as objects defying gravity or passing through each other.
There is a disconnect between visual realism and accurate physical interactions in AI-generated videos.
Existing taxonomies primarily focus on technical implementation details, lacking a framework to evaluate models based on their deeper understanding of physical laws.
results: Current video generation models, even state-of-the-art approaches, still fall short of human-level physics understanding, particularly in complex scenarios involving multiple interacting objects or fluid dynamics.

Evaluation benchmarks like PhyBench and VideoPhy reveal consistent failures in physical consistency, highlighting the limitations of models reliant solely on visual patterns or pre-established knowledge.

The survey identifies key challenges including limited physical coverage, computational inefficiency, the sim2real gap, and difficulties with multi-object interactions, guiding future research directions like physics foundation models and neuro-symbolic approaches.
solution: Lin et al. propose a cognitive science-inspired framework to categorize video generation models based on their physics understanding, drawing parallels to Piaget's stages of cognitive development.
The survey provides a detailed taxonomy of physical phenomena (mechanics, optics, thermal, material properties) essential for comprehensive physics cognition in video generation.

It reviews current technologies and approaches within each cognitive tier, from motion-guided generation and geometric constraints to 3D-aware physics simulation, LLM-based reasoning, and differentiable physics engines.
paper_summary.summary: This survey by Lin et al. introduces a cognitive science-inspired framework that classifies video generation models based on their physics cognition, ranging from basic pattern perception to active world simulation. The work details the evolution of these models and evaluates their current capabilities against various physical phenomena, finding that even advanced systems struggle with comprehensive and accurate physical understanding.

title: Exploring the Evolution of Physics Cognition in Video Generation: A  Survey

abstract: Understanding and replicating the real world is a critical challenge in Artificial General Intelligence (AGI) research. To achieve this, many existing approaches, such as world models, aim to capture the fundamental principles governing the physical world, enabling more accurate simulations and meaningful interactions. However, current methods often treat different modalities, including 2D (images), videos, 3D, and 4D representations, as independent domains, overlooking their interdependencies. Additionally, these methods typically focus on isolated dimensions of reality without systematically integrating their connections. In this survey, we present a unified survey for multimodal generative models that investigate the progression of data dimensionality in real-world simulation. Specifically, this survey starts from 2D generation (appearance), then moves to video (appearance+dynamics) and 3D generation (appearance+geometry), and finally culminates in 4D generation that integrate all dimensions. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first attempt to systematically unify the study of 2D, video, 3D and 4D generation within a single framework. To guide future research, we provide a comprehensive review of datasets, evaluation metrics and future directions, and fostering insights for newcomers. This survey serves as a bridge to advance the study of multimodal generative models and real-world simulation within a unified framework.

organization_info.name: The Chinese University of Hong Kong
The Hong Kong University of Science and Technology (Guangzhou)
Shanghai Academy of AI for Science

title: Simulating the Real World: A Unified Survey of Multimodal Generative Models

abstract: The concept of world models has garnered significant attention due to advancements in multimodal large language models such as GPT-4 and video generation models such as Sora, which are central to the pursuit of artificial general intelligence. This survey offers a comprehensive review of the literature on world models. Generally, world models are regarded as tools for either understanding the present state of the world or predicting its future dynamics. This review presents a systematic categorization of world models, emphasizing two primary functions: (1) constructing internal representations to understand the mechanisms of the world, and (2) predicting future states to simulate and guide decision-making. Initially, we examine the current progress in these two categories. We then explore the application of world models in key domains, including generative games, autonomous driving, robotics, and social simulacra, with a focus on how each domain utilizes these aspects. Finally, we outline key challenges and provide insights into potential future research directions. We summarize the representative papers along with their code repositories in this https URL.

keyInsights: World models primarily serve two functions: constructing implicit internal representations of world mechanisms for informed decision-making, and explicitly simulating and forecasting future states of the world, often through visual data.

Large Language Models (LLMs) and Multimodal LLMs (MLLMs) are emerging as powerful backbones for acquiring and leveraging extensive latent 'world knowledge' encompassing common sense, spatial, temporal, and social understanding.

The field is rapidly moving towards creating fully interactive and embodied 3D simulations, shifting from passive video generation to physically constrained, first-person, action-conditioned future world predictions.
originalProblem: Existing literature lacked a systematic definition of world models and a comprehensive analysis of their diverse requirements across applications.
A persistent debate exists regarding whether a world model's core function is to understand the current world state or primarily to predict future states for decision-making.
Recent capabilities of large multimodal AI models necessitate a re-evaluation of what constitutes a 'world model' and how current AI systems align with this concept.
results: Provided a comprehensive overview of world model applications, demonstrating their potential in game intelligence, embodied intelligence (robotics), urban intelligence (autonomous driving), and societal intelligence (social simulacra).

Identified critical open problems for world models, including ensuring robust physical rule adherence, enriching the social dimension, developing standardized benchmarks, and bridging the simulation-to-reality gap.
Highlighted the need to address ethical and safety concerns related to data privacy, the potential for simulating unsafe scenarios, and accountability for AI-generated content.
solution: Introduced a novel categorization of world models based on two primary functions: constructing implicit representations to understand the external world and predicting future states to simulate and guide decision-making.
Contextualized modern world model advancements within a historical narrative, tracing their evolution from early cognitive theories to contemporary deep learning paradigms.

Systematically examined how these dual functionalities of world models are leveraged across diverse application domains, including generative games, embodied intelligence, urban intelligence, and societal intelligence.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from Tsinghua University present a comprehensive survey of world models, proposing a dual categorization based on understanding implicit world representations and predicting future states. The work analyzes world model applications across generative games, robotics, autonomous driving, and social simulacra, while outlining critical open challenges.

title: Understanding World or Predicting Future? A Comprehensive Survey of World Models

abstract: Generative artificial intelligence has recently progressed from static image and video synthesis to 3D content generation, culminating in the emergence of 4D generation-the task of synthesizing temporally coherent dynamic 3D assets guided by user input. As a burgeoning research frontier, 4D generation enables richer interactive and immersive experiences, with applications ranging from digital humans to autonomous driving. Despite rapid progress, the field lacks a unified understanding of 4D representations, generative frameworks, basic paradigms, and the core technical challenges it faces. This survey provides a systematic and in-depth review of the 4D generation landscape. To comprehensively characterize 4D generation, we first categorize fundamental 4D representations and outline associated techniques for 4D generation. We then present an in-depth analysis of representative generative pipelines based on conditions and representation methods. Subsequently, we discuss how motion and geometry priors are integrated into 4D outputs to ensure spatio-temporal consistency under various control schemes. From an application perspective, this paper summarizes 4D generation tasks in areas such as dynamic object/scene generation, digital human synthesis, editable 4D content, and embodied AI. Furthermore, we summarize and multi-dimensionally compare four basic paradigms for 4D generation: End-to-End, Generated-Data-Based, Implicit-Distillation-Based, and Explicit-Supervision-Based. Concluding our analysis, we highlight five key challenges-consistency, controllability, diversity, efficiency, and fidelity-and contextualize these with current this http URL distilling recent advances and outlining open problems, this work offers a comprehensive and forward-looking perspective to guide future research in 4D generation.

keyInsights: 4D generation primarily utilizes Mesh, NeRF, Point-cloud, and Gaussian Splatting representations, with 4D Gaussian Splatting emerging as an efficient and high-quality option for dynamic scenes.
Diffusion Models and Score Distillation Sampling (SDS) are foundational techniques, providing the underlying generative power and effective optimization strategies for 4D content.

The field has diversified into four generative paradigms—End-to-End, Generated-Data-Based, Implicit-Distillation-Based, and Explicit-Supervision-Based—each presenting distinct trade-offs in efficiency, quality, and controllability.
originalProblem: Existing generative models struggle with long-range temporal coherence and flexible user control when synthesizing dynamic 3D assets.
The rapidly evolving field of 4D generation lacks a unified understanding, structured taxonomy, and comprehensive overview of its diverse advancements.
There is a growing demand for richer interactive and immersive experiences in applications like digital humans and AR/VR, requiring dynamic, view-consistent 3D content.
results: Five core technical challenges consistently impede 4D generation quality and utility: consistency, controllability, diversity, efficiency, and fidelity.
A growing trend towards multi-conditional 4D generation leverages video inputs and diverse multimodal controls to achieve enhanced precision and flexibility.

Three critical open problems were identified: the scarcity of large-scale multimodal 4D datasets, the need for unified and efficient model frameworks, and the absence of comprehensive evaluation benchmarks.
solution: Conducted an extensive literature review to categorize 4D generation methods based on representations (Mesh, NeRF, Point Cloud, Gaussian Splatting) and foundational techniques (Diffusion Models, SDS).
Proposed a unified taxonomy based on conditioning signals (Text, Image, Video, 3D, Multi-modal) and distilled four representative generative paradigms.
Identified and discussed key technical challenges and future research directions, including the critical need for large-scale datasets and comprehensive benchmarks.
paper_summary.summary: A comprehensive survey on 4D generation by researchers at Zhejiang University and University of Nevada, Reno, systematically reviews techniques, highlights the pivotal role of Diffusion Models and 4D Gaussian Splatting, and identifies five core technical challenges, including spatio-temporal consistency and computational efficiency.

title: Advances in 4D Generation: A Survey

abstract:
Motion generation, the task of synthesizing realistic motion sequences from various conditioning inputs, has become a central problem in computer vision, computer graphics, and robotics, with applications ranging from animation and virtual agents to human-robot interaction. As the field has rapidly progressed with the introduction of diverse modeling paradigms including GANs, autoencoders, autoregressive models, and diffusion-based techniques, each approach brings its own advantages and limitations. This growing diversity has created a need for a comprehensive and structured review that specifically examines recent developments from the perspective of the generative approach employed.
In this survey, we provide an in-depth categorization of motion generation methods based on their underlying generative strategies. Our main focus is on papers published in top-tier venues since 2023, reflecting the most recent advancements in the field. In addition, we analyze architectural principles, conditioning mechanisms, and generation settings, and compile a detailed overview of the evaluation metrics and datasets used across the literature. Our objective is to enable clearer comparisons and identify open challenges, thereby offering a timely and foundational reference for researchers and practitioners navigating the rapidly evolving landscape of motion generation.

keyInsights: Diffusion models constitute nearly 40% of recent motion generation methods, establishing them as the predominant generative paradigm for synthesizing diverse, high-quality motion.
Attention-based architectures are overwhelmingly preferred, utilized in over 70% of surveyed papers, underscoring their effectiveness in capturing long-range spatio-temporal dependencies.

Critical challenges persist in achieving physical plausibility (e.g., avoiding foot sliding and penetrations), ensuring generalization across diverse characters, and standardizing evaluation metrics for fair comparisons.
originalProblem: The field of motion generation has experienced rapid diversification and progress in generative modeling, making it challenging for researchers to keep pace with developments.
Existing surveys were often broad in scope or outdated due to the accelerated pace of innovation in generative AI, particularly since 2023.
A lack of a structured review categorized by underlying generative approach hindered effective comparison and assessment of different methodologies.
results: Statistical analysis identified HumanML3D and KIT-ML as the most frequently used datasets, while text-based conditioning emerged as the primary control signal for motion generation.
The survey detailed current architectural preferences, showing a strong favoritism towards transformers, and noted the continued relevance of convolutional networks and RNNs for specific tasks.

Emerging research directions include a focus on real-time generation, integrating richer multi-modal conditioning, incorporating physics-informed modeling, and developing foundation models that unify various motion tasks.
solution: The researchers performed a systematic literature review focused on top-tier publications since 2023, classifying methods based on their core generative strategies (e.g., diffusion, autoencoders, GANs, autoregressive models).
They conducted a multi-dimensional analysis, examining model architectures, conditioning mechanisms, and specific application domains for each identified generative approach.

A comprehensive compilation of commonly used datasets and evaluation metrics was provided, complemented by quantitative statistical analysis of trends in approaches, architectures, datasets, and conditioning signals.
paper_summary.summary: Autodesk Research conducted a comprehensive review of motion generation, systematically categorizing recent methods by their underlying generative approach and compiling prevalent benchmarks. The study revealed the dominance of diffusion models and attention-based architectures, while highlighting persistent challenges in physical realism and evaluation standardization.

title: Motion Generation: A Survey of Generative Approaches and Benchmarks

abstract: The landscape of video generation is shifting, from a focus on generating visually appealing clips to building virtual environments that support interaction and maintain physical plausibility. These developments point toward the emergence of video foundation models that function not only as visual generators but also as implicit world models, models that simulate the physical dynamics, agent-environment interactions, and task planning that govern real or imagined worlds. This survey provides a systematic overview of this evolution, conceptualizing modern video foundation models as the combination of two core components: an implicit world model and a video renderer. The world model encodes structured knowledge about the world, including physical laws, interaction dynamics, and agent behavior. It serves as a latent simulation engine that enables coherent visual reasoning, long-term temporal consistency, and goal-driven planning. The video renderer transforms this latent simulation into realistic visual observations, effectively producing videos as a "window" into the simulated world. We trace the progression of video generation through four generations, in which the core capabilities advance step by step, ultimately culminating in a world model, built upon a video generation model, that embodies intrinsic physical plausibility, real-time multimodal interaction, and planning capabilities spanning multiple spatiotemporal scales. For each generation, we define its core characteristics, highlight representative works, and examine their application domains such as robotics, autonomous driving, and interactive gaming. Finally, we discuss open challenges and design principles for next-generation world models, including the role of agent intelligence in shaping and evaluating these systems. An up-to-date list of related works is maintained at this link.

keyInsights: Modern video foundation models are increasingly acting as implicit world models, encoding structured knowledge and physical dynamics as latent simulation engines, rather than merely rendering visuals.

The evolution of world models progresses through stages from superficial visual fidelity, to consistent faithfulness and semantic interactiveness, and ultimately to complex, real-time task planning and stochasticity modeling.

Comprehensive world models hold the potential to profoundly impact scientific research across various disciplines and enhance machine intelligence by providing virtually unlimited training trajectories and interaction data.
originalProblem: The concept of a "world model" lacks a clear, unified definition within AI, particularly in relation to video generation.
There is an absence of a systematic taxonomy and evaluation framework to track and categorize the progression of video generation capabilities towards comprehensive world modeling.
A structured understanding is needed for how advancements in end-to-end video generation contribute to and enable the development of interactive, physically plausible world models.
results: The proposed taxonomy outlines four generations: Generation 1 (Faithfulness) for basic visual quality, Generation 2 (Interactiveness) for controllable simulation, Generation 3 (Planning) for real-time and complex prediction, and Generation 4 (Stochasticity) for modeling low-probability and multi-scale events.
Analysis places most current state-of-the-art models in video generation within Generation 2, demonstrating consistent faithfulness and semantic/navigational interactiveness.

The roadmap projects future capabilities including "physically intrinsic faithfulness" and "stochasticity planning," aiming for simulations of arbitrary physical laws and the generation of diverse, plausible outcomes such as parallel universes.
solution: Introduces a four-generation taxonomy that systematically categorizes the evolution of video generation models into world models, based on core capabilities: faithfulness, interactiveness, and planning.
Provides a formal definition of a "physical world model" as a digital simulation engine embedded with comprehensive world knowledge, capable of predicting future scenes based on environmental states.

Formally defines "navigation modes" by criteria of temporality, content independence, and spatial reasoning, distinguishing them from static spatial conditions to clarify control signals for interactive models.
paper_summary.summary: The paper proposes a four-generation taxonomy and a roadmap for the evolution of video generation models into artificial intelligence-driven world models. It categorizes existing and future systems based on their advancing capabilities in visual faithfulness, interactiveness, and complex planning, aiming for comprehensive simulations that can predict stochastic, multi-scale events.

title: Simulating the Visual World with Artificial Intelligence: A Roadmap

abstract:
Generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) has rapidly advanced the field of
computer vision by enabling machines to create and interpret visual data with
unprecedented sophistication. This transformation builds upon a foundation of
generative models to produce realistic images, videos, and 3D/4D content.
Conventional generative models primarily focus on visual fidelity while often
neglecting the physical plausibility of the generated content. This gap limits
their effectiveness in applications that require adherence to real-world
physical laws, such as robotics, autonomous systems, and scientific
simulations. As generative models evolve to increasingly integrate physical
realism and dynamic simulation, their potential to function as "world
simulators" expands. Therefore, the field of physics-aware generation in
computer vision is rapidly growing, calling for a comprehensive survey to
provide a structured analysis of current efforts. To serve this purpose, the
survey presents a systematic review, categorizing methods based on how they
incorporate physical knowledge, either through explicit simulation or implicit
learning. It also analyzes key paradigms, discusses evaluation protocols, and
identifies future research directions. By offering a comprehensive overview,
this survey aims to help future developments in physically grounded generation
for computer vision. The reviewed papers are summarized at
this https URL

keyInsights: Generative AI is moving beyond visual realism towards functionally plausible and physically coherent content, crucial for applications like robotics and autonomous systems.
Explicit integration of physical simulation is the dominant approach to achieve robust physical realism in generated content.
Evaluating physics awareness requires specialized benchmarks and metrics beyond conventional visual fidelity, as existing methods often fail to detect physical inconsistencies.
originalProblem: Generative AI models in computer vision often produce visually realistic content that lacks physical plausibility, failing to adhere to real-world physical laws.
This limitation restricts their utility in applications requiring physical accuracy, such as robotics, autonomous systems, and scientific simulations.
There was a need for a systematic and comprehensive survey to map and categorize the emerging field of physics-aware generative AI.
results: A clear distinction between explicit (PAG-E) and implicit (PAG-I) physics-aware generation is established.
Six distinct paradigms are identified for explicit physics integration, detailing how generative models interact with physical simulators.
The survey highlights an increasing reliance on automatic and LLM-based methods for inferring physical parameters, moving beyond manual input.
It confirms that conventional visual fidelity metrics are insufficient for assessing physical awareness, necessitating the development of specialized benchmarks despite challenges in standardization.
solution: Defines 'Physics-Aware Generation' (PAG) and categorizes it into explicit (PAG-E) and implicit (PAG-I) approaches.
Establishes a comprehensive taxonomy of six paradigms for integrating explicit physical simulation into generative models, detailing their interaction with physics engines.
Systematically reviews the components necessary for physics-aware generation, including physical materials, simulation methods, and parameter inference.
paper_summary.summary: This survey systematically reviews and categorizes generative models in computer vision that produce physically plausible outputs, establishing a taxonomy of explicit and implicit physics-aware generation methods. It details six paradigms for integrating physical simulation, revealing a trend towards functional realism, and identifies current challenges in evaluation metrics.

title: Generative Physical AI in Vision: A Survey

abstract:
Video generation is a rapidly advancing research area, garnering significant attention due to its broad range of applications. One critical aspect of this field is the generation of long-duration videos, which presents unique challenges and opportunities. This paper presents the first survey of recent advancements in long video generation and summarises them into two key paradigms: divide and conquer temporal autoregressive.
We delve into the common models employed in each paradigm, including aspects of network design and conditioning techniques. Furthermore, we offer a comprehensive overview and classification of the datasets and evaluation metrics which are crucial for advancing long video generation research. Concluding with a summary of existing studies, we also discuss the emerging challenges and future directions in this dynamic field. We hope that this survey will serve as an essential reference for researchers and practitioners in the realm of long video generation.

organization_info.name: State Key Lab of Integrated Service Networks
The Engineerin Research Center of Trusted Digital Economy
Shannxi Key Laboratory of Blockchain and Secure Computing

keyInsights: A standardized definition for "long video" is crucial for fostering consistent research and evaluation within the community.

Most current long video generation methods fall into either the "Divide and Conquer" paradigm, which handles complexity by breaking it down, or the "Temporal Autoregressive" paradigm, which maintains consistency by building sequentially.
Resource constraints (computational, memory, data scarcity) and the intricate challenge of maintaining temporal consistency over long durations are central bottlenecks in the field.
originalProblem: Lack of a consistent and standardized definition for "long video" across different research studies, hindering comparative analysis.
Significant computational and memory demands, coupled with a scarcity of suitable long video datasets, impede the development of robust models.
Challenges in maintaining temporal consistency, content continuity, and visual diversity over extended video durations.
results: Established a formal definition of "long video" as content exceeding 10 seconds in duration or comprising more than 100 frames at 10fps.
Categorized long video generation methods into two main paradigms: "Divide and Conquer" for hierarchical synthesis and "Temporal Autoregressive" for sequential, context-dependent generation.

Identified key strategies for improving photo-realistic quality by focusing on temporal-spatial consistency, content continuity, and diversity, alongside practical methods for resource optimization and dataset augmentation.
solution: Proposing a standardized definition for "long video" (exceeding 10 seconds or 100 frames at 10fps) to unify research terminology and benchmarking.
Systematically classifying existing long video generation methodologies into two dominant paradigms: "Divide and Conquer" and "Temporal Autoregressive."
Analyzing various techniques for quality enhancement, resource management, and identifying key challenges and promising future research directions.
paper_summary.summary: This survey establishes the first comprehensive overview of long video generation, proposing a standardized definition for "long video" and systematically categorizing existing methods, challenges, and future research directions. It consolidates diverse techniques for synthesizing extended video sequences, analyzing their approaches to quality enhancement and resource management.

title: A Survey on Long Video Generation: Challenges, Methods, and Prospects

abstract: The advent of text-driven 360-degree panorama generation, enabling the synthesis of 360-degree panoramic images directly from textual descriptions, marks a transformative advancement in immersive visual content creation. This innovation significantly simplifies the traditionally complex process of producing such content. Recent progress in text-to-image diffusion models has accelerated the rapid development in this emerging field. This survey presents a comprehensive review of text-driven 360-degree panorama generation, offering an in-depth analysis of state-of-the-art algorithms. We extend our analysis to two closely related domains: text-driven 360-degree 3D scene generation and text-driven 360-degree panoramic video generation. Furthermore, we critically examine current limitations and propose promising directions for future research. A curated project page with relevant resources and research papers is available at this https URL.

keyInsights: Different panoramic representations (Equirectangular, Cubemap, Multi-Perspective) present distinct trade-offs in distortion handling and compatibility with standard 2D generative models.

Generative frameworks exhibit inherent trade-offs between speed and global consistency, with non-autoregressive methods being faster but challenging coherence, while autoregressive methods improve consistency but are slower.

The adaptation of pre-trained 2D text-to-image models to 360-degree generation involves a spectrum of strategies, from costly full fine-tuning to parameter-efficient or training-free methods, each with varying performance and efficiency.
originalProblem: Creating high-quality, immersive 360-degree panoramas traditionally requires specialized equipment and expertise, posing a bottleneck for the escalating demand from VR/AR technologies.
The rapidly progressing field of text-driven 360-degree panorama generation lacked a dedicated and comprehensive survey to consolidate research efforts and clarify methodologies.

Generating 360-degree content presents unique technical challenges, including maintaining geometric consistency, seamless visual coherence, and mitigating distortions across a full spherical field of view.
results: A comprehensive taxonomy categorizes text-driven 360-degree panorama generation methods into Text-Only Generation and Text-Driven NFoV Outpainting, further detailing their underlying mechanisms.

Comparative analysis revealed that while text-only methods like PanFusion achieve high perceptual quality, Text-Driven NFoV Outpainting approaches such as Diffusion360 (NFoV version) demonstrate superior efficiency and lower error rates across panorama-specific metrics.

The survey identified critical challenges, including the need for improved evaluation metrics and higher resolution generation, alongside emerging directions like text-driven 360-degree 3D scene and video generation.
solution: The authors conducted the first dedicated and comprehensive survey on text-driven 360-degree panorama generation, consolidating existing knowledge and providing a structured taxonomy.
The survey systematically reviews state-of-the-art methods, categorizing them into Text-Only Generation and Text-Driven Narrow Field-of-View (NFoV) Outpainting paradigms.

It establishes a foundational understanding by explaining panoramic representations (e.g., Equirectangular, Cubemap), specialized datasets, and both universal and panorama-specific evaluation metrics.
paper_summary.summary: A comprehensive survey systematically reviews text-driven 360-degree panorama generation, establishing a taxonomy of methods and analyzing representational challenges while benchmarking state-of-the-art models. The work also reveals the inadequacy of universal evaluation metrics for panoramic content and proposes future research directions.

title: A Survey on Text-Driven 360-Degree Panorama Generation

abstract:
Sora is a text-to-video generative AI model, released by OpenAI in February
2024. The model is trained to generate videos of realistic or imaginative
scenes from text instructions and show potential in simulating the physical
world. Based on public technical reports and reverse engineering, this paper
presents a comprehensive review of the model's background, related
technologies, applications, remaining challenges, and future directions of
text-to-video AI models. We first trace Sora's development and investigate the
underlying technologies used to build this "world simulator". Then, we describe
in detail the applications and potential impact of Sora in multiple industries
ranging from film-making and education to marketing. We discuss the main
challenges and limitations that need to be addressed to widely deploy Sora,
such as ensuring safe and unbiased video generation. Lastly, we discuss the
future development of Sora and video generation models in general, and how
advancements in the field could enable new ways of human-AI interaction,
boosting productivity and creativity of video generation.

keyInsights: Scaling laws observed in large language models also apply to large vision models, with Sora demonstrating emergent abilities in complex video generation.
The diffusion transformer architecture, combined with advanced data processing via 'spacetime latent patches', provides a robust foundation for generating high-fidelity, long-duration videos.
Sora's demonstrated capabilities, such as 3D consistency and object persistence, represent a step towards developing AI systems that can function as 'world simulators'.
originalProblem: Generating long, high-quality, and coherent videos from text instructions was technically challenging due to the inherent temporal complexity of video.
Existing text-to-video models were limited to producing short, often low-fidelity clips, which restricted their utility for complex or extended narratives.
Advancing AI's ability to understand and interact with the physical world in motion remained a key, long-term research mission for developing more capable AI systems.
results: Sora generates videos up to one minute in length while maintaining high visual quality and strong adherence to complex user text instructions.
The model exhibits emergent simulation abilities, including 3D consistency, dynamic camera motion, and object persistence across video sequences.
Sora can train on and generate videos with variable durations, resolutions, and aspect ratios at their native sizes, enhancing output composition and framing.
Identified limitations include occasional inconsistencies in physical realism, such as inaccurate depiction of cause-and-effect or rigid body dynamics.
solution: The model employs a pre-trained diffusion transformer as its core architecture for video generation.
Raw video inputs are likely processed by a jointly trained spatial-temporal VAE encoder to create lower-dimensional 'spacetime latent patches', efficiently handling diverse video formats.

Robust instruction following is achieved through a 'caption improvement' method, involving a specialized video captioner to generate highly descriptive captions for training data, potentially augmented by LLMs like GPT-4V for prompt expansion.
paper_summary.summary: An independent analysis of OpenAI's Sora details the text-to-video generative AI model, highlighting its capabilities to produce up to one-minute-long, high-quality videos based on human instructions. The review, based on public reports and reverse engineering, describes the model's likely diffusion transformer architecture and emergent world simulation abilities.

title:
Sora: A Review on Background, Technology, Limitations, and Opportunities
  of Large Vision Models

abstract: The rapid evolution of video generation has enabled models to simulate complex physical dynamics and long-horizon causalities, positioning them as potential world simulators. However, a critical gap still remains between the theoretical capacity for world simulation and the heavy computational costs of spatiotemporal modeling. To address this, we comprehensively and systematically review video generation frameworks and techniques that consider efficiency as a crucial requirement for practical world modeling. We introduce a novel taxonomy in three dimensions: efficient modeling paradigms, efficient network architectures, and efficient inference algorithms. We further show that bridging this efficiency gap directly empowers interactive applications such as autonomous driving, embodied AI, and game simulation. Finally, we identify emerging research frontiers in efficient video-based world modeling, arguing that efficiency is a fundamental prerequisite for evolving video generators into general-purpose, real-time, and robust world simulators.

keyInsights: Efficiency is a fundamental prerequisite, not merely an optimization, for video generation models to evolve into practical, real-time world simulators.
A multi-faceted approach, integrating efficiency across modeling paradigms, network architectures, and inference algorithms, is essential to address the high computational costs.
The structured taxonomy clarifies the landscape of techniques, helping researchers understand existing efforts and identify future opportunities for advancing world modeling.
originalProblem: Video generation models, despite their potential as "World Simulators" for understanding physical laws, face significant computational and memory demands.
The high dimensionality of video data and complexity of physically-based dynamics impede scalability and real-time interaction for practical world modeling.
A systematic framework or review for organizing and understanding efficiency-focused techniques specifically in video-based world models was absent.
results: Systematically identified the critical efficiency gap hindering the practical deployment of video generation models as world simulators.
Proposed a novel three-dimensional taxonomy that comprehensively categorizes efficiency improvement techniques across modeling, architecture, and inference.
Showcased how the surveyed efficiency techniques directly empower and enhance key applications in autonomous driving, embodied AI, and interactive world simulation.
solution: Provides a comprehensive, systematic review of efficient video generation frameworks and techniques relevant for world modeling.
Introduces a novel three-dimensional taxonomy for organizing efficiency methods: efficient modeling paradigms, network architectures, and inference algorithms.
Systematically categorizes and discusses specific techniques within each dimension, such as diffusion distillation, sparse attention, and quantization.
paper_summary.summary: This review systematically surveys efficient video generation frameworks and techniques crucial for developing video-based world models, introducing a novel three-dimensional taxonomy across modeling paradigms, network architectures, and inference algorithms. It examines how these methods enable practical applications such as autonomous driving, embodied AI, and interactive world simulation by mitigating computational costs.

title: Video Generation Models as World Models: Efficient Paradigms, Architectures and Algorithms

abstract: With the recent advancement in large language models (LLMs), there is a growing interest in combining LLMs with multimodal learning. Previous surveys of multimodal large language models (MLLMs) mainly focus on multimodal understanding. This survey elaborates on multimodal generation and editing across various domains, comprising image, video, 3D, and audio. Specifically, we summarize the notable advancements with milestone works in these fields and categorize these studies into LLM-based and CLIP/T5-based methods. Then, we summarize the various roles of LLMs in multimodal generation and exhaustively investigate the critical technical components behind these methods and the multimodal datasets utilized in these studies. Additionally, we dig into tool-augmented multimodal agents that can leverage existing generative models for human-computer interaction. Lastly, we discuss the advancements in the generative AI safety field, investigate emerging applications, and discuss future prospects. Our work provides a systematic and insightful overview of multimodal generation and processing, which is expected to advance the development of Artificial Intelligence for Generative Content (AIGC) and world models. A curated list of all related papers can be found at this https URL

author_info.institution: Hong Kong University of Science and Technology

organization_info.name: The Chinese University of Hong Kong

keyInsights: Large Language Models (LLMs) are fundamentally shifting multimodal generation and editing from basic conditioning to advanced prompt understanding, reasoning, and interactive capabilities.

LLMs adopt diverse and specialized roles across modalities, acting as layout planners for images, temporal prompt generators for videos, semantic guides for 3D content, and coordinated backbones for audio.
The rise of LLMs as orchestrators for tool-augmented multimodal agents represents a new paradigm for complex, interactive content creation, further extending their utility beyond text.
originalProblem: Prior surveys on Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) largely concentrated on multimodal understanding, neglecting the rapidly evolving areas of generation and editing.
The explosive growth of LLMs created a need for a systematic framework to understand their application in generating and modifying diverse non-textual content.
Researchers lacked a unified overview of how LLMs enhance multimodal content creation across modalities like images, videos, 3D, and audio.
results: The survey documented a clear paradigm shift from CLIP/T5-based methods to LLM-dominant approaches, which enable more controllable and interactive multimodal content creation.
It elucidated how LLMs fulfill diverse, specialized roles (e.g., planners, synthesizers, backbones) that enhance generation and editing across images, videos, 3D, and audio.

The research highlighted the critical emergence of LLM-orchestrated multimodal agents and underscored the increasing importance of generative AI safety concerns, including bias, copyright, and fabricated content.
solution: The authors conducted a systematic review, categorizing methods into pre-LLM (CLIP/T5-based) and LLM-based approaches for multimodal generation and editing across various modalities.
They detailed the diverse roles LLMs assume in these generative pipelines, such as layout planners, prompt synthesizers, backbones, or orchestrators for external tools.
The survey also examined key technical components, relevant datasets, and dedicated sections to tool-augmented multimodal agents and generative AI safety.
paper_summary.summary: This survey systematically reviews how Large Language Models (LLMs) are integrated into multimodal generation and editing across images, videos, 3D content, and audio. It categorizes existing approaches, details the diverse roles LLMs play in these processes, and outlines critical safety considerations and future research directions.

title: LLMs Meet Multimodal Generation and Editing: A Survey

abstract: In the rapidly advancing realm of visual generation, diffusion models have revolutionized the landscape, marking a significant shift in capabilities with their impressive text-guided generative functions. However, relying solely on text for conditioning these models does not fully cater to the varied and complex requirements of different applications and scenarios. Acknowledging this shortfall, a variety of studies aim to control pre-trained text-to-image (T2I) models to support novel conditions. In this survey, we undertake a thorough review of the literature on controllable generation with T2I diffusion models, covering both the theoretical foundations and practical advancements in this domain. Our review begins with a brief introduction to the basics of denoising diffusion probabilistic models (DDPMs) and widely used T2I diffusion models. We then reveal the controlling mechanisms of diffusion models, theoretically analyzing how novel conditions are introduced into the denoising process for conditional generation. Additionally, we offer a detailed overview of research in this area, organizing it into distinct categories from the condition perspective: generation with specific conditions, generation with multiple conditions, and universal controllable generation. For an exhaustive list of the controllable generation literature surveyed, please refer to our curated repository at this https URL.
