title: Controllable Generation with Text-to-Image Diffusion Models: A Survey

abstract: Video generation models, as one form of world models, have emerged as one of the most exciting frontiers in AI, promising agents the ability to imagine the future by modeling the temporal evolution of complex scenes. In autonomous driving, this vision gives rise to driving world models: generative simulators that imagine ego and agent futures, enabling scalable simulation, safe testing of corner cases, and rich synthetic data generation. Yet, despite fast-growing research activity, the field lacks a rigorous benchmark to measure progress and guide priorities. Existing evaluations remain limited: generic video metrics overlook safety-critical imaging factors; trajectory plausibility is rarely quantified; temporal and agent-level consistency is neglected; and controllability with respect to ego conditioning is ignored. Moreover, current datasets fail to cover the diversity of conditions required for real-world deployment. To address these gaps, we present DrivingGen, the first comprehensive benchmark for generative driving world models. DrivingGen combines a diverse evaluation dataset curated from both driving datasets and internet-scale video sources, spanning varied weather, time of day, geographic regions, and complex maneuvers, with a suite of new metrics that jointly assess visual realism, trajectory plausibility, temporal coherence, and controllability. Benchmarking 14 state-of-the-art models reveals clear trade-offs: general models look better but break physics, while driving-specific ones capture motion realistically but lag in visual quality. DrivingGen offers a unified evaluation framework to foster reliable, controllable, and deployable driving world models, enabling scalable simulation, planning, and data-driven decision-making.
title: DrivingGen: A Comprehensive Benchmark for Generative Video World Models in Autonomous Driving

abstract: Generative AI has experienced remarkable growth in recent years, leading to a wide array of applications across diverse domains. In this paper, we present a comprehensive survey of more than 350 generative AI applications, providing a structured taxonomy and concise descriptions of various unimodal and even multimodal generative AIs. The survey is organized into sections, covering a wide range of unimodal generative AI applications such as text, images, video, gaming and brain information. Our survey aims to serve as a valuable resource for researchers and practitioners to navigate the rapidly expanding landscape of generative AI, facilitating a better understanding of the current state-of-the-art and fostering further innovation in the field.
title: A survey of Generative AI Applications

abstract:
Generative AI is reshaping art, gaming, and most notably animation. Recent
breakthroughs in foundation and diffusion models have reduced the time and cost
of producing animated content. Characters are central animation components,
involving motion, emotions, gestures, and facial expressions. The pace and
breadth of advances in recent months make it difficult to maintain a coherent
view of the field, motivating the need for an integrative review. Unlike
earlier overviews that treat avatars, gestures, or facial animation in
isolation, this survey offers a single, comprehensive perspective on all the
main generative AI applications for character animation. We begin by examining
the state-of-the-art in facial animation, expression rendering, image
synthesis, avatar creation, gesture modeling, motion synthesis, object
generation, and texture synthesis. We highlight leading research, practical
deployments, commonly used datasets, and emerging trends for each area. To
support newcomers, we also provide a comprehensive background section that
introduces foundational models and evaluation metrics, equipping readers with
the knowledge needed to enter the field. We discuss open challenges and map
future research directions, providing a roadmap to advance AI-driven
character-animation technologies. This survey is intended as a resource for
researchers and developers entering the field of generative AI animation or
adjacent fields. Resources are available at:
this https URL

organization_info.name: Iran University of Science and Technology

keyInsights: Generative AI, including diffusion models, transformers, and GANs, is being applied across the entire character animation pipeline, from design to rendering.
The field is moving towards unified, multimodal approaches that integrate various AI techniques to handle complex tasks like audio-driven facial animation or text-to-3D avatar generation.
Significant progress has been made in automating labor-intensive aspects of animation, enabling higher quality results with less manual effort.
originalProblem: Traditional character animation is a labor-intensive process requiring specialized artistic and technical skills.
Previous research in AI for animation was fragmented, focusing on specific subdomains without a unified view of the entire pipeline.
Achieving realistic and diverse animations for facial expressions, full body motion, and character creation was challenging and time-consuming.
results: Generative AI models enable the creation of highly detailed textures, realistic movements, and diverse character variations using techniques like diffusion models.
Systems can now generate realistic facial animations from audio or text inputs and synthesize full 3D avatars from textual descriptions or single images.
AI models successfully produce plausible body gestures and motion sequences, often driven by text or audio, while incorporating physical constraints.
solution: The paper surveys the application of generative AI techniques, such as Diffusion Models, Transformer-based models, and GANs, to various character animation components.
It synthesizes developments across facial animation, avatar creation, gesture modeling, and motion synthesis, providing a holistic perspective on AI's role.
The research details how specific AI architectures excel at different tasks, for instance, diffusion models for high-quality visuals and transformers for sequential motion data.
paper_summary.summary: This survey paper from Sharif University of Technology, Iran University of Science and Technology, and Qatar Computing Research Institute presents a comprehensive overview of how generative artificial intelligence techniques are transforming character animation. It integrates advances across previously fragmented areas like facial animation, avatar creation, and motion synthesis, demonstrating how AI can automate and augment traditional animation processes, leading to more accessible and efficient content creation.

title: Generative AI for Character Animation: A Comprehensive Survey of  Techniques, Applications, and Future Directions

abstract: The recent wave of AI-generated content (AIGC) has witnessed substantial success in computer vision, with the diffusion model playing a crucial role in this achievement. Due to their impressive generative capabilities, diffusion models are gradually superseding methods based on GANs and auto-regressive Transformers, demonstrating exceptional performance not only in image generation and editing, but also in the realm of video-related research. However, existing surveys mainly focus on diffusion models in the context of image generation, with few up-to-date reviews on their application in the video domain. To address this gap, this paper presents a comprehensive review of video diffusion models in the AIGC era. Specifically, we begin with a concise introduction to the fundamentals and evolution of diffusion models. Subsequently, we present an overview of research on diffusion models in the video domain, categorizing the work into three key areas: video generation, video editing, and other video understanding tasks. We conduct a thorough review of the literature in these three key areas, including further categorization and practical contributions in the field. Finally, we discuss the challenges faced by research in this domain and outline potential future developmental trends. A comprehensive list of video diffusion models studied in this survey is available at this https URL.
title: A Survey on Video Diffusion Models

abstract: Future Frame Synthesis (FFS), the task of generating subsequent video frames from context, represents a core challenge in machine intelligence and a cornerstone for developing predictive world models. This survey provides a comprehensive analysis of the FFS landscape, charting its critical evolution from deterministic algorithms focused on pixel-level accuracy to modern generative paradigms that prioritize semantic coherence and dynamic plausibility. We introduce a novel taxonomy organized by algorithmic stochasticity, which not only categorizes existing methods but also reveals the fundamental drivers--advances in architectures, datasets, and computational scale--behind this paradigm shift. Critically, our analysis identifies a bifurcation in the field's trajectory: one path toward efficient, real-time prediction, and another toward large-scale, generative world simulation. By pinpointing key challenges and proposing concrete research questions for both frontiers, this survey serves as an essential guide for researchers aiming to advance the frontiers of visual dynamic modeling.

keyInsights: Future Frame Synthesis (FFS) has evolved from deterministic methods, which often produce blurry outputs due to the perception-distortion trade-off, to stochastic and generative approaches that prioritize semantic coherence and dynamic plausibility.
The progression in FFS is driven by advancements in model architectures (e.g., Transformers, Diffusion Models, LLMs), the availability of larger datasets, and increased computational scale.
The field is bifurcating into two main paths: efficient, real-time prediction for practical applications and large-scale, generative world simulation aimed at building general-purpose AI models.
originalProblem: The field of Future Frame Synthesis (FFS) lacked a unified, comprehensive overview that bridges traditional deterministic video prediction with modern generative AI approaches.
Prior FFS methods, often deterministic, struggled with producing blurry outputs and failed to model the inherent uncertainty and diverse possibilities in future video frames.
A clear framework was needed to categorize and understand the rapid evolution of FFS, from pixel-level accuracy to semantic coherence and long-term generation.
results: A novel three-tiered taxonomy for FFS methods was established based on their 'degree of algorithmic stochasticity' (deterministic, stochastic, generative), providing a structured understanding of the field's evolution.
The survey identified architectural advancements, dataset scaling, and computational scale as fundamental drivers behind the paradigm shift in FFS.

Persistent challenges in FFS were highlighted, including the inadequacy of traditional evaluation metrics for generative models, difficulties in long-term synthesis and generalization, and the 'tokenizer bottleneck' in token-based models.
solution: The authors conduct a comprehensive survey of Future Frame Synthesis (FFS), systematically tracing its evolution from early deterministic algorithms to contemporary generative paradigms.
A novel classification framework is introduced, categorizing FFS approaches by their 'degree of algorithmic stochasticity' (deterministic, stochastic, and generative).
The survey identifies and analyzes key drivers of progress, universal challenges, and distinct future research frontiers within the FFS domain.
paper_summary.summary: This survey provides a comprehensive analysis of Future Frame Synthesis (FFS), detailing its evolution from deterministic prediction to generative modeling. It introduces a new taxonomy based on algorithmic stochasticity and identifies the architectural, data, and computational advancements driving the field, noting a bifurcation into real-time applications and generative world simulation.

title: A Survey on Future Frame Synthesis: Bridging Deterministic and Generative Approaches

abstract: In recent years, generative artificial intelligence has achieved significant advancements in the field of image generation, spawning a variety of applications. However, video generation still faces considerable challenges in various aspects, such as controllability, video length, and richness of details, which hinder the application and popularization of this technology. In this work, we propose a controllable video generation framework, dubbed MimicMotion, which can generate high-quality videos of arbitrary length mimicking specific motion guidance. Compared with previous methods, our approach has several highlights. Firstly, we introduce confidence-aware pose guidance that ensures high frame quality and temporal smoothness. Secondly, we introduce regional loss amplification based on pose confidence, which significantly reduces image distortion. Lastly, for generating long and smooth videos, we propose a progressive latent fusion strategy. By this means, we can produce videos of arbitrary length with acceptable resource consumption. With extensive experiments and user studies, MimicMotion demonstrates significant improvements over previous approaches in various aspects. Detailed results and comparisons are available on our project page: this https URL .

keyInsights: Modulating pose guidance with confidence scores from pose estimators effectively mitigates the impact of noisy inputs, leading to clearer and more reliable control signals.

Targeted amplification of training loss in specific, perceptually important regions (like hands) when pose confidence is high can significantly improve local image quality and reduce common distortions.

A carefully designed progressive latent fusion scheme with position-aware weighting is essential for generating arbitrarily long videos while maintaining seamless temporal consistency and preventing flickering artifacts across segment transitions.
originalProblem: Existing pose-guided human video generation models often produce imagery distortion, especially in hands, and face a trade-off between visual detail and temporal smoothness.
Inaccurate or noisy pose estimations in dynamic videos frequently lead to conflicts with temporal smoothness, resulting in visual artifacts and hindering model training.

Generating high-quality, consistent videos of arbitrary length is challenging due to computational limits, error accumulation in autoregressive methods, and abrupt transitions in segment-based fusion techniques.
results: MimicMotion achieved superior quantitative results on the TikTok dataset, with the lowest FID-VID (9.3), FVD (594), and highest SSIM (0.795) and PSNR (20.1) compared to state-of-the-art baselines.
Qualitative evaluations demonstrated improved imagery quality, particularly for human hands, and enhanced temporal smoothness with significantly reduced flickering.

A user study confirmed strong subjective preference for MimicMotion, with participants preferring its generated videos over MagicPose (96.3%), Moore (98.6%), and MuseV (75.5%) regarding image quality and temporal smoothness.
solution: Introduces confidence-aware pose guidance that modulates the brightness of keypoints and limbs on the pose map based on their confidence scores, making the guidance more robust to noisy inputs.
Implements hand region enhancement by identifying high-confidence hand keypoint regions and amplifying their loss during training, which directs the model to focus on generating higher quality hands.

Develops a progressive latent fusion strategy for long video generation, splitting sequences into overlapping segments and using a position-aware weighting scheme to smoothly blend latent features across segment boundaries, ensuring temporal coherence.
paper_summary.summary: MimicMotion presents a framework for high-quality, controllable human motion video generation of arbitrary length from a single reference image and a pose sequence. The method integrates confidence-aware pose guidance, regional loss amplification, and progressive latent fusion to achieve superior visual fidelity and temporal coherence, outperforming existing models on standard benchmarks and user preference.

title: MimicMotion: High-Quality Human Motion Video Generation with Confidence-aware Pose Guidance

abstract: This paper offers an insightful examination of how currently top-trending AI technologies, i.e., generative artificial intelligence (Generative AI) and large language models (LLMs), are reshaping the field of video technology, including video generation, understanding, and streaming. It highlights the innovative use of these technologies in producing highly realistic videos, a significant leap in bridging the gap between real-world dynamics and digital creation. The study also delves into the advanced capabilities of LLMs in video understanding, demonstrating their effectiveness in extracting meaningful information from visual content, thereby enhancing our interaction with videos. In the realm of video streaming, the paper discusses how LLMs contribute to more efficient and user-centric streaming experiences, adapting content delivery to individual viewer preferences. This comprehensive review navigates through the current achievements, ongoing challenges, and future possibilities of applying Generative AI and LLMs to video-related tasks, underscoring the immense potential these technologies hold for advancing the field of video technology related to multimedia, networking, and AI communities.

keyInsights: Generative AI models like diffusion models are driving advanced video synthesis, editing, and prediction, yet face hurdles in achieving temporal consistency and managing high computational costs.
LLMs enhance video understanding through improved captioning, question answering, and retrieval capabilities, despite ongoing challenges with temporal reasoning and multimodal data integration.

LLMs are optimizing video streaming by enabling better bandwidth and viewport prediction, compression, and resource allocation, but a unified framework and comprehensive datasets remain underdeveloped.

The pervasive integration of Generative AI and LLMs in video technology necessitates urgent attention to ethical concerns, including misinformation from deepfakes, intellectual property violations, privacy risks, and algorithmic bias.
originalProblem: The research fields of video generation, understanding, and streaming historically progressed in separate silos.
A need for a holistic analysis of how both Generative AI and LLMs collectively influence the entire video lifecycle, from creation to interpretation and delivery.
Existing technical challenges, such as long-term video synthesis consistency, fine-grained control, and efficient content delivery, required consolidation and forward-looking guidance.
results: Provided a foundational and comprehensive understanding of the current state-of-the-art in integrating Generative AI and LLMs with video technology.

Identified specific technical hurdles across video generation (e.g., temporal consistency), understanding (e.g., multimodal fusion), and streaming (e.g., unified framework), guiding future research directions.

Highlighted critical societal and ethical concerns, such as misinformation from deepfakes, privacy leakage, and content censorship, urging interdisciplinary collaboration for responsible AI development.
solution: Systematically collected, examined, and synthesized over 100 relevant papers from prominent academic databases and internet research.
Categorized and analyzed the applications of Generative AI models (GANs, VAEs, Autoregressive, Diffusion) and LLMs across video generation, understanding, and streaming.
Structured the findings into specific technologies, key applications, and persistent challenges within each domain, alongside cross-cutting ethical and societal concerns.
paper_summary.summary: A comprehensive survey details the integrated applications of Generative AI and Large Language Models (LLMs) in video generation, understanding, and streaming, providing a structured overview of current methods, real-world applications, and the technical and ethical challenges faced.

title: A Survey on Generative AI and LLM for Video Generation, Understanding, and Streaming

abstract: Human motion video generation has garnered significant research interest due to its broad applications, enabling innovations such as photorealistic singing heads or dynamic avatars that seamlessly dance to music. However, existing surveys in this field focus on individual methods, lacking a comprehensive overview of the entire generative process. This paper addresses this gap by providing an in-depth survey of human motion video generation, encompassing over ten sub-tasks, and detailing the five key phases of the generation process: input, motion planning, motion video generation, refinement, and output. Notably, this is the first survey that discusses the potential of large language models in enhancing human motion video generation. Our survey reviews the latest developments and technological trends in human motion video generation across three primary modalities: vision, text, and audio. By covering over two hundred papers, we offer a thorough overview of the field and highlight milestone works that have driven significant technological breakthroughs. Our goal for this survey is to unveil the prospects of human motion video generation and serve as a valuable resource for advancing the comprehensive applications of digital humans. A complete list of the models examined in this survey is available in Our Repository this https URL.

organization_info.name: Nanjing University of Science and Technology
Guangdong Laboratory of Artificial Intelligence and Digital Economy (SZ)

keyInsights: Large Language Models (LLMs) represent a promising, though currently underutilized, paradigm for semantically rich and controllable motion planning, moving beyond implicit feature mapping.
Diffusion Models (DMs), especially Latent Diffusion Models (LDMs), have become the dominant generative framework for achieving high-quality and diverse human motion video outputs.

Despite significant advancements, common visual artifacts persist across state-of-the-art methods, including issues with fidelity (e.g., hand blur, facial distortion), consistency (identity, background), and realism of movement.
originalProblem: Existing surveys on human motion video generation are fragmented, focusing on individual methods or specific sub-tasks rather than a holistic, end-to-end process.
The complex, multi-stage nature of human motion video generation lacks a unified framework to connect different sub-tasks and advancements.
The potential role of Large Language Models (LLMs) in enhancing human motion video generation, particularly for motion planning, has not been systematically reviewed in prior HMVG surveys.
results: Established a unified five-phase pipeline for understanding the entire human motion video generation process, encompassing input to final output.

Identified two primary strategies for integrating LLMs into motion planning: generating fine-grained motion descriptions for retrieval and projecting descriptions into a latent space to guide generative models.

Conducted a quantitative comparative analysis of nine open-source methods on the TikTok dataset, providing empirical performance metrics (e.g., SSIM, PSNR, FID-VID) and outlining persistent visual artifacts.
solution: Decomposed human motion video generation into a novel five-phase pipeline: Input, Motion Planning, Motion Video Generation, Refinement, and Output.
Categorized over 200 papers based on three primary input modalities (audio, text, vision) and their multimodal combinations, offering a structured analysis of advancements.
Pioneered the discussion of Large Language Models (LLMs) for motion planning, identifying both retrieval-based and generative strategies for their integration.
paper_summary.summary: This paper provides the first comprehensive, end-to-end survey of human motion video generation, introducing a novel five-phase pipeline and pioneering the discussion of Large Language Models (LLMs) for motion planning. It reviews over 200 papers, categorizes sub-tasks by input modality, and conducts a quantitative comparison of state-of-the-art methods.

title: Human Motion Video Generation: A Survey

abstract: The rapid advancement of diffusion models has greatly improved video synthesis, especially in controllable video generation, which is vital for applications like autonomous driving. Although DiT with 3D VAE has become a standard framework for video generation, it introduces challenges in controllable driving video generation, especially for geometry control, rendering existing control methods ineffective. To address these issues, we propose MagicDrive-V2, a novel approach that integrates the MVDiT block and spatial-temporal conditional encoding to enable multi-view video generation and precise geometric control. Additionally, we introduce an efficient method for obtaining contextual descriptions for videos to support diverse textual control, along with a progressive training strategy using mixed video data to enhance training efficiency and generalizability. Consequently, MagicDrive-V2 enables multi-view driving video synthesis with $3.3\times$ resolution and $4\times$ frame count (compared to current SOTA), rich contextual control, and geometric controls. Extensive experiments demonstrate MagicDrive-V2's ability, unlocking broader applications in autonomous driving.

keyInsights: Frame-wise geometric controls can be effectively integrated with 3D VAE compressed latents through carefully designed temporal downsampling and embedding mechanisms within the conditional encoding modules.

A progressive training approach combining stages of image, short video, and mixed-length/resolution long video generation significantly enhances training efficiency and allows models to extrapolate to unseen video lengths and resolutions.

Rich contextual control can be achieved by augmenting sparse dataset captions with MLLM-generated descriptions, enabling diverse scene attributes like weather and time of day to be controlled via text prompts without conflicting with explicit geometric conditions.
originalProblem: Existing methods for driving video generation were constrained by both low resolution and limited frame counts, impeding their utility for detailed autonomous driving applications.

Integrating frame-wise geometric control (e.g., object positions, ego-vehicle trajectories) with scalable Diffusion in Transformers (DiT) models that use 3D Variational Autoencoders (VAEs) was an open challenge due to spatio-temporal compression.
Autonomous driving datasets often have limited textual descriptions, restricting the diversity of text-guided scene generation and contextual control over videos.
results: Generates multi-view driving videos at an unprecedented 848x1600 resolution and up to 241 frames (approx. 20 seconds) in a single inference, representing a 3.3x increase in resolution and 4x increase in frame count compared to prior methods.
Achieves a Frechet Video Distance (FVD) of 94.84, outperforming previous state-of-the-art (MagicDrive's 218.12), indicating superior video quality and realism.

Demonstrates precise and adaptive control over road maps, 3D bounding boxes, and ego-vehicle trajectories, confirmed by higher mAP for 3D object detection and mIoU for BEV segmentation on generated content, while also generalizing to diverse textual conditions.
solution: Utilizes a DiT architecture with STDiT-3 blocks and a CogVAE for efficient spatial-temporal compression, enabling scalable generation of high-resolution and long videos.

Introduces novel spatial-temporal conditional encoding modules for road maps, 3D bounding boxes, and ego-vehicle trajectories to precisely align geometric controls with the 3D VAE's compressed latent space.
Employs a multi-stage progressive training strategy with mixed video data and uses a Multimodal Large Language Model (MLLM) to generate richer contextual captions for enhanced text control.
paper_summary.summary: MagicDrive-V2, developed by researchers from CUHK, HKUST, and Huawei Noah's Ark Lab, generates high-resolution, long, multi-view street-view videos with precise adaptive control for autonomous driving applications. It leverages a DiT and 3D VAE with novel spatial-temporal conditional encoding, achieving 3.3x resolution and 4x frame count over prior methods while demonstrating superior realism and control fidelity.

title: MagicDrive-V2: High-Resolution Long Video Generation for Autonomous Driving with Adaptive Control

abstract: Diffusion generative models have recently become a powerful technique for creating and modifying high-quality, coherent video content. This survey provides a comprehensive overview of the critical components of diffusion models for video generation, including their applications, architectural design, and temporal dynamics modeling. The paper begins by discussing the core principles and mathematical formulations, then explores various architectural choices and methods for maintaining temporal consistency. A taxonomy of applications is presented, categorizing models based on input modalities such as text prompts, images, videos, and audio signals. Advancements in text-to-video generation are discussed to illustrate the state-of-the-art capabilities and limitations of current approaches. Additionally, the survey summarizes recent developments in training and evaluation practices, including the use of diverse video and image datasets and the adoption of various evaluation metrics to assess model performance. The survey concludes with an examination of ongoing challenges, such as generating longer videos and managing computational costs, and offers insights into potential future directions for the field. By consolidating the latest research and developments, this survey aims to serve as a valuable resource for researchers and practitioners working with video diffusion models. Website: this https URL

keyInsights: Temporal consistency is the most critical and complex challenge unique to video diffusion, primarily addressed through spatio-temporal attention, temporal upsampling, and hierarchical generation.
Latent Diffusion Models (LDMs) operating in a compressed latent space have become the standard, significantly improving computational efficiency and enabling higher resolution video generation.

Video diffusion models are increasingly explored beyond content creation for applications in intelligent decision-making, such as world models for planning and generating synthetic training data for robotics.
originalProblem: The rapidly accelerating field of video diffusion models is fragmented, making it difficult for researchers and practitioners to keep pace with new publications and understand diverse approaches.
A lack of consolidated resources impedes researchers from understanding the underlying principles, architectural trade-offs, and unresolved issues in video diffusion technology.
Addressing the unique challenges of temporal consistency in video generation requires a systematic overview of current methods and architectural adaptations.
results: The survey synthesizes that diffusion models are now the dominant technique for high-quality video generation, outperforming previous GANs and auto-regressive transformers.

It identifies UNets and Vision Transformers as primary architectural backbones, with Latent Diffusion Models (LDMs) being the de facto standard for efficiency and Cascaded Diffusion Models (CDMs) for resolution scaling.

The paper highlights the versatility of these models across diverse applications, including unconditional, text-to-video, image-to-video generation, video editing, and multi-modal synthesis, while also addressing their computational costs and ethical implications.
solution: The authors provide a comprehensive survey detailing the fundamental principles, mathematical formulations (DDPM, SBM), and architectural designs adapted for video generation.
They categorize applications based on input modalities (text-to-video, image-to-video, video completion) and review notable papers within each category.

The survey summarizes training datasets, strategies (training from scratch, fine-tuning pre-trained image models), and evaluation metrics (FID, FVD, KVD, FVMD, IS, CLIP similarity) to provide practical guidance.
paper_summary.summary: This survey from Bielefeld University, UBC, and the University of Waterloo offers a structured overview of video diffusion models, detailing their core principles, architectural choices, and diverse applications. It highlights how these models address the complexities of temporal consistency in video generation and identifies key challenges and future directions, including their emerging role in intelligent decision-making.

title: Video Diffusion Models: A Survey

abstract: Human video generation is a dynamic and rapidly evolving task that aims to synthesize 2D human body video sequences with generative models given control conditions such as text, audio, and pose. With the potential for wide-ranging applications in film, gaming, and virtual communication, the ability to generate natural and realistic human video is critical. Recent advancements in generative models have laid a solid foundation for the growing interest in this area. Despite the significant progress, the task of human video generation remains challenging due to the consistency of characters, the complexity of human motion, and difficulties in their relationship with the environment. This survey provides a comprehensive review of the current state of human video generation, marking, to the best of our knowledge, the first extensive literature review in this domain. We start with an introduction to the fundamentals of human video generation and the evolution of generative models that have facilitated the field's growth. We then examine the main methods employed for three key sub-tasks within human video generation: text-driven, audio-driven, and pose-driven motion generation. These areas are explored concerning the conditions that guide the generation process. Furthermore, we offer a collection of the most commonly utilized datasets and the evaluation metrics that are crucial in assessing the quality and realism of generated videos. The survey concludes with a discussion of the current challenges in the field and suggests possible directions for future research. The goal of this survey is to offer the research community a clear and holistic view of the advancements in human video generation, highlighting the milestones achieved and the challenges that lie ahead.

organization_info.name: Hong Kong University of Science and Technology (Guangzhou)

keyInsights: Human video generation methods can be primarily categorized by their conditional signals: text-driven, audio-driven, and pose-driven approaches.
Many human video generation systems employ a two-stage pipeline, first converting the input signal into a motion or pose representation and then translating this into a video.
Persistent challenges include handling occlusion, maintaining appearance consistency, ensuring anatomical correctness, and generating temporally coherent movements.
originalProblem: A lack of comprehensive, dedicated surveys focusing specifically on full-body human video generation, as existing reviews either covered general video synthesis or niche subdomains like talking-head generation.
The need for a structured understanding of the diverse techniques, conditional signals, challenges, and applications within the rapidly evolving field of human video generation.
results: The survey classifies human video generation methods into text-driven, audio-driven, and pose-driven categories, detailing the approaches, subcategories, and common pipelines within each.
A comprehensive list of technical challenges in human video generation is identified, including issues with occlusion handling, body deformation, appearance inconsistency, and temporal misalignment.
The review provides an inventory of commonly used datasets and evaluation metrics, and outlines promising future research directions like multimodal conditioning and 3D-aware generation.
solution: Conducted the first comprehensive survey specifically dedicated to full-body or half-body human video generation, including hands and faces.
Organized the analysis based on conditional signals (text, audio, pose) to systematically review diverse generation techniques.
Identified and discussed key technical challenges, existing datasets, evaluation metrics, and future research directions to provide a structured overview of the field.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from Hong Kong University of Science and Technology (Guangzhou) present the first comprehensive survey on human video generation, specifically focusing on full-body synthesis. The work categorizes methods by conditional signals like text, audio, and pose, detailing techniques, persistent challenges, and future research directions in the field.

title: A Comprehensive Survey on Human Video Generation: Challenges, Methods, and Insights

abstract:
Recent years have witnessed remarkable advances in artificial intelligence
generated content(AIGC), with diverse input modalities, e.g., text, image,
video, audio and 3D. The 3D is the most close visual modality to real-world 3D
environment and carries enormous knowledge. The 3D content generation shows
both academic and practical values while also presenting formidable technical
challenges. This review aims to consolidate developments within the burgeoning
domain of 3D content generation. Specifically, a new taxonomy is proposed that
categorizes existing approaches into three types: 3D native generative methods,
2D prior-based 3D generative methods, and hybrid 3D generative methods. The
survey covers approximately 60 papers spanning the major techniques. Besides,
we discuss limitations of current 3D content generation techniques, and point
out open challenges as well as promising directions for future work.
Accompanied with this survey, we have established a project website where the
resources on 3D content generation research are provided. The project page is
available at https://github.com/hitcslj/Awesome-AIGC-3D.

organization_info.name: The Chinese University of Hong Kong

keyInsights: The field has progressed through three major paradigms: 3D native methods limited by data scarcity, 2D prior-based methods leveraging large 2D models, and hybrid methods combining the strengths of both.

Despite advancements, critical challenges persist in achieving production-level quality (geometry, texture), precise controllability over generated assets, and improving generation efficiency and speed.

Future progress in 3D AIGC requires focused efforts on collecting massive, high-quality 3D datasets, developing scalable 3D foundation models, and establishing robust, automated, human-aligned evaluation benchmarks.
originalProblem: Rapid and fragmented developments in 3D Artificial Intelligence Generated Content (AIGC) across various methodologies (3D native, 2D prior-based, hybrid).
The urgent need for a comprehensive and structured overview to help researchers and practitioners navigate the rapidly evolving domain.
Lack of a unified resource consolidating current techniques, their limitations, and promising future research directions in 3D content generation.
results: A new taxonomy was established, categorizing 3D content generation into 3D native, 2D prior-based, and hybrid methods, further segmented by content type (objects, scenes, human avatars).

Approximately 60 milestone papers were systematically reviewed, demonstrating the progression from data-constrained 3D-only approaches to sophisticated hybrid methods focusing on fidelity and dynamics.

Critical challenges in 3D content quality, controllability, and efficiency were identified, along with concrete future research directions concerning data acquisition, model architectures, and robust evaluation metrics.
solution: Proposed a novel and systematic taxonomy to categorize the latest advancements in 3D content generation methods into three distinct paradigms.
Conducted an exhaustive review and analysis of approximately 60 significant papers, illustrating their main ideas, contributions, and evolutionary trajectories.
Identified and discussed the limitations of current techniques, articulated open technical challenges, and pointed towards concrete avenues for future research.
paper_summary.summary: This survey paper by Liu et al. systematically categorizes and reviews recent advancements in 3D content generation, proposing a new taxonomy that spans 3D native, 2D prior-based, and hybrid generative methods. It consolidates research from approximately 60 milestone papers, outlining critical challenges and future directions for the field.

title: A Comprehensive Survey on 3D Content Generation

abstract: General world models represent a crucial pathway toward achieving Artificial General Intelligence (AGI), serving as the cornerstone for various applications ranging from virtual environments to decision-making systems. Recently, the emergence of the Sora model has attained significant attention due to its remarkable simulation capabilities, which exhibits an incipient comprehension of physical laws. In this survey, we embark on a comprehensive exploration of the latest advancements in world models. Our analysis navigates through the forefront of generative methodologies in video generation, where world models stand as pivotal constructs facilitating the synthesis of highly realistic visual content. Additionally, we scrutinize the burgeoning field of autonomous-driving world models, meticulously delineating their indispensable role in reshaping transportation and urban mobility. Furthermore, we delve into the intricacies inherent in world models deployed within autonomous agents, shedding light on their profound significance in enabling intelligent interactions within dynamic environmental contexts. At last, we examine challenges and limitations of world models, and discuss their potential future directions. We hope this survey can serve as a foundational reference for the research community and inspire continued innovation. This survey will be regularly updated at: this https URL.

organization_info.name: EVOL Lab, Institute of AI, China Telecom

keyInsights: General World Models (GWMs), through their generative and predictive capabilities, represent a fundamental pathway towards achieving Artificial General Intelligence (AGI).
OpenAI's Sora demonstrates emergent properties, including an "incipient comprehension of physical laws," indicating its trajectory towards functioning as a world simulator.
Scaling laws, similar to those in Large Language Models, appear to apply to visual foundation models, where increased scale leads to more sophisticated emergent abilities.
originalProblem: The rapidly evolving field of general world models (GWMs) lacked a unified, comprehensive overview that integrated recent advancements and diverse applications.
The capabilities of advanced generative models, particularly OpenAI's Sora, prompted a need to critically evaluate their potential as "world simulators."
Researchers required a structured analysis to understand the role of GWMs in achieving Artificial General Intelligence (AGI) across various domains.
results: The survey confirms Sora's capability to generate high-fidelity, physically consistent video, indicating an implicit understanding of world dynamics at scale.
It details how world models are integrated into autonomous driving for neural simulators, 3D scene prediction, and 4D pre-training, enhancing safety and efficiency.
For autonomous agents, world models facilitate "learning in imagination," significantly boosting learning efficiency and performance in complex environments.
The analysis highlights persistent challenges in causal reasoning, adherence to physical laws, generalization, and computational efficiency, along with ethical concerns in deployment.
solution: The authors compiled a comprehensive survey analyzing existing literature on general world models and their applications.
They structured the analysis by categorizing GWMs into three primary application domains: video generation, autonomous driving, and autonomous agents.
The survey deeply examined the core generative technologies, representative models, and inferred architectures, including an analysis of Sora's potential design.
paper_summary.summary: This survey comprehensively maps the field of general world models (GWMs), critically examining how models like Sora demonstrate nascent capabilities in simulating physical worlds and outlining their applications across video generation, autonomous driving, and intelligent agents. It identifies GWMs as a crucial pathway to Artificial General Intelligence while detailing remaining challenges and future directions.

title: Is Sora a World Simulator? A Comprehensive Survey on General World Models and Beyond

abstract: The rapid advancement of Large AI Models (LAIMs), particularly diffusion models and large language models, has marked a new era where AI-generated multimedia is increasingly integrated into various aspects of daily life. Although beneficial in numerous fields, this content presents significant risks, including potential misuse, societal disruptions, and ethical concerns. Consequently, detecting multimedia generated by LAIMs has become crucial, with a marked rise in related research. Despite this, there remains a notable gap in systematic surveys that focus specifically on detecting LAIM-generated multimedia. Addressing this, we provide the first survey to comprehensively cover existing research on detecting multimedia (such as text, images, videos, audio, and multimodal content) created by LAIMs. Specifically, we introduce a novel taxonomy for detection methods, categorized by media modality, and aligned with two perspectives: pure detection (aiming to enhance detection performance) and beyond detection (adding attributes like generalizability, robustness, and interpretability to detectors). Additionally, we have presented a brief overview of generation mechanisms, public datasets, online detection tools, and evaluation metrics to provide a valuable resource for researchers and practitioners in this field. Most importantly, we offer a focused analysis from a social media perspective to highlight their broader societal impact. Furthermore, we identify current challenges in detection and propose directions for future research that address unexplored, ongoing, and emerging issues in detecting multimedia generated by LAIMs. Our aim for this survey is to fill an academic gap and contribute to global AI security efforts, helping to ensure the integrity of information in the digital realm. The project link is this https URL.
title: Detecting Multimedia Generated by Large AI Models: A Survey

abstract:
AI-generated content (AIGC) methods aim to produce text, images, videos, 3D
assets, and other media using AI algorithms. Due to its wide range of
applications and the potential of recent works, AIGC developments -- especially
in Machine Learning (ML) and Deep Learning (DL) -- have been attracting
significant attention, and this survey focuses on comprehensively reviewing
such advancements in ML/DL. AIGC methods have been developed for various data
modalities, such as image, video, text, 3D shape, 3D scene, 3D human avatar, 3D
motion, and audio -- each presenting unique characteristics and challenges.
Furthermore, there have been significant developments in cross-modality AIGC
methods, where generative methods receive conditioning input in one modality
and produce outputs in another. Examples include going from various modalities
to image, video, 3D, and audio. This paper provides a comprehensive review of
AIGC methods across different data modalities, including both single-modality
and cross-modality methods, highlighting the various challenges, representative
works, and recent technical directions in each setting. We also survey the
representative datasets throughout the modalities, and present comparative
results for various modalities. Moreover, we discuss the typical applications
of AIGC methods in various domains, challenges, and future research directions.

organization_info.name: Singapore University of Technology and Design

title: AI-Generated Content (AIGC) for Various Data Modalities: A Survey

abstract: The rapid advancement of AI technology, particularly in generating AI-generated content (AIGC), has transformed numerous fields, e.g., art video generation, but also brings new risks, including the misuse of AI for misinformation and intellectual property theft. To address these concerns, AIGC watermarks offer an effective solution to mitigate malicious activities. However, existing watermarking surveys focus more on traditional watermarks, overlooking AIGC-specific challenges. In this work, we propose a systematic investigation into AIGC watermarking and provide the first formal definition of AIGC watermarking. Different from previous surveys, we provide a taxonomy based on the core properties of the watermark which are summarized through comprehensive literature from various AIGC modalities. Derived from the properties, we discuss the functionality and security threats of AIGC watermarking. In the end, we thoroughly investigate the AIGC governance of different countries and practitioners. We believe this taxonomy better aligns with the practical demands for watermarking in the era of GenAI, thus providing a clearer summary of existing work and uncovering potential future research directions for the community.

organization_info.name: Hangzhou High-Tech Zone (Binjiang) Institute of Blockchain and Data Security
The State Key Laboratory of Blockchain and Data Security, Zhejiang University

title: SoK: On the Role and Future of AIGC Watermarking in the Era of Gen-AI

abstract: Generative AI is transforming image synthesis, enabling the creation of high-quality, diverse, and photorealistic visuals across industries like design, media, healthcare, and autonomous systems. Advances in techniques such as image-to-image translation, text-to-image generation, domain transfer, and multimodal alignment have broadened the scope of automated visual content creation, supporting a wide spectrum of applications. These advancements are driven by models like Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs), conditional frameworks, and diffusion-based approaches such as Stable Diffusion. This work presents a structured classification of image generation techniques based on the nature of the input, organizing methods by input modalities like noisy vectors, latent representations, and conditional inputs. We explore the principles behind these models, highlight key frameworks including DALL-E, ControlNet, and DeepSeek Janus-Pro, and address challenges such as computational costs, data biases, and output alignment with user intent. By offering this input-centric perspective, this study bridges technical depth with practical insights, providing researchers and practitioners with a comprehensive resource to harness generative AI for real-world applications.
title: Generative AI for Vision: A Comprehensive Study of Frameworks and Applications

abstract: The burgeoning field of Artificial Intelligence Generated Content (AIGC) is witnessing rapid advancements, particularly in video generation. This paper introduces AIGCBench, a pioneering comprehensive and scalable benchmark designed to evaluate a variety of video generation tasks, with a primary focus on Image-to-Video (I2V) generation. AIGCBench tackles the limitations of existing benchmarks, which suffer from a lack of diverse datasets, by including a varied and open-domain image-text dataset that evaluates different state-of-the-art algorithms under equivalent conditions. We employ a novel text combiner and GPT-4 to create rich text prompts, which are then used to generate images via advanced Text-to-Image models. To establish a unified evaluation framework for video generation tasks, our benchmark includes 11 metrics spanning four dimensions to assess algorithm performance. These dimensions are control-video alignment, motion effects, temporal consistency, and video quality. These metrics are both reference video-dependent and video-free, ensuring a comprehensive evaluation strategy. The evaluation standard proposed correlates well with human judgment, providing insights into the strengths and weaknesses of current I2V algorithms. The findings from our extensive experiments aim to stimulate further research and development in the I2V field. AIGCBench represents a significant step toward creating standardized benchmarks for the broader AIGC landscape, proposing an adaptable and equitable framework for future assessments of video generation tasks. We have open-sourced the dataset and evaluation code on the project website: this https URL.

keyInsights: Current I2V algorithms generally lack fine-grained control, struggling to generate video content with precise textual descriptions or specific motion directions.
Mainstream I2V models are limited in generating long videos (typically around 3 seconds), highlighting challenges in maintaining temporal consistency across extended sequences.
Video generation remains computationally intensive with slow inference speeds, requiring optimization for practical applications.
originalProblem: Existing Image-to-Video (I2V) benchmarks lack diverse, open-domain datasets representative of real-world scenarios.
There is an absence of unified and comprehensive evaluation metrics to consistently assess I2V algorithms across key aspects like control-video alignment, motion, and temporal consistency.
The disparities in datasets and metrics prevent equitable comparison and understanding of different state-of-the-art I2V algorithms.
results: Closed-source models, Pika and Gen2, consistently demonstrated superior generation effects across all dimensions, with Gen2 achieving the best image fidelity and longest video generation (up to 96 frames), and Pika excelling in localized motion and temporal consistency.

Stable Video Diffusion (SVD) emerged as the best-performing open-source algorithm, offering balanced capabilities across control-video alignment, motion effects, temporal consistency, and video quality.
The user study involving 42 individuals confirmed a strong correlation between AIGCBench's automated evaluation metrics and human judgment, validating the benchmark's effectiveness.
solution: Developed AIGCBench, a scalable benchmark featuring a dataset of 3,928 test cases, combining real-world video-text/image-text pairs with synthetically generated high-quality image-text pairs using GPT-4 and Stable Diffusion.

Proposed a unified evaluation framework with 11 metrics across four crucial dimensions: control-video alignment, motion effects, temporal consistency, and video quality, integrating both reference-based and video-free approaches.

Evaluated five state-of-the-art I2V algorithms, including open-source (VideoCrafter, I2VGen-XL, SVD) and closed-source (Pika, Gen2) models, under consistent conditions and validated findings with a user study.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from the Chinese Academy of Sciences introduced AIGCBench, a comprehensive benchmark for Image-to-Video (I2V) content, integrating a diverse dataset and 11 evaluation metrics across four dimensions. It establishes a standardized framework for assessing I2V algorithms, with automated metrics demonstrating strong correlation with human judgment.

title: AIGCBench: Comprehensive Evaluation of Image-to-Video Content Generated by AI

abstract: The evolution of video generation from text, from animating MNIST to simulating the world with Sora, has progressed at a breakneck speed. Here, we systematically discuss how far text-to-video generation technology supports essential requirements in world modeling. We curate 250+ studies on text-based video synthesis and world modeling. We then observe that recent models increasingly support spatial, action, and strategic intelligences in world modeling through adherence to completeness, consistency, invention, as well as human interaction and control. We conclude that text-to-video generation is adept at world modeling, although homework in several aspects, such as the diversity-consistency trade-offs, remains to be addressed.
title: Sora as a World Model? A Complete Survey on Text-to-Video Generation

abstract: With the rapid advancement and widespread adoption of VR/AR technologies, there is a growing demand for the creation of high-quality, immersive dynamic scenes. However, existing generation works predominantly concentrate on the creation of static scenes or narrow perspective-view dynamic scenes, falling short of delivering a truly 360-degree immersive experience from any viewpoint. In this paper, we introduce \textbf{TiP4GEN}, an advanced text-to-dynamic panorama scene generation framework that enables fine-grained content control and synthesizes motion-rich, geometry-consistent panoramic 4D scenes. TiP4GEN integrates panorama video generation and dynamic scene reconstruction to create 360-degree immersive virtual environments. For video generation, we introduce a \textbf{Dual-branch Generation Model} consisting of a panorama branch and a perspective branch, responsible for global and local view generation, respectively. A bidirectional cross-attention mechanism facilitates comprehensive information exchange between the branches. For scene reconstruction, we propose a \textbf{Geometry-aligned Reconstruction Model} based on 3D Gaussian Splatting. By aligning spatial-temporal point clouds using metric depth maps and initializing scene cameras with estimated poses, our method ensures geometric consistency and temporal coherence for the reconstructed scenes. Extensive experiments demonstrate the effectiveness of our proposed designs and the superiority of TiP4GEN in generating visually compelling and motion-coherent dynamic panoramic scenes. Our project page is at this https URL.

organization_info.name: The University of Texas at Austin
Visual Intelligence + X International Joint Laboratory

title: TiP4GEN: Text to Immersive Panorama 4D Scene Generation

abstract: World models and video generation are pivotal technologies in the domain of autonomous driving, each playing a critical role in enhancing the robustness and reliability of autonomous systems. World models, which simulate the dynamics of real-world environments, and video generation models, which produce realistic video sequences, are increasingly being integrated to improve situational awareness and decision-making capabilities in autonomous vehicles. This paper investigates the relationship between these two technologies, focusing on how their structural parallels, particularly in diffusion-based models, contribute to more accurate and coherent simulations of driving scenarios. We examine leading works such as JEPA, Genie, and Sora, which exemplify different approaches to world model design, thereby highlighting the lack of a universally accepted definition of world models. These diverse interpretations underscore the field's evolving understanding of how world models can be optimized for various autonomous driving tasks. Furthermore, this paper discusses the key evaluation metrics employed in this domain, such as Chamfer distance for 3D scene reconstruction and Fréchet Inception Distance (FID) for assessing the quality of generated video content. By analyzing the interplay between video generation and world models, this survey identifies critical challenges and future research directions, emphasizing the potential of these technologies to jointly advance the performance of autonomous driving systems. The findings presented in this paper aim to provide a comprehensive understanding of how the integration of video generation and world models can drive innovation in the development of safer and more reliable autonomous vehicles.

organization_info.name: Nanjing University of Science and Technology

keyInsights: The concept of 'world models' lacks a universally accepted definition, demonstrating diverse interpretations across leading AI systems like Meta's JEPA, Google's Genie, and OpenAI's Sora.

Significant structural similarities exist between diffusion-based video generation models and autonomous driving world models, particularly in their two-stage autoencoder/perception and generative/prediction module architectures.

These architectural commonalities are crucial for enhancing the performance and capabilities of autonomous driving systems by facilitating realistic scene generation and robust environmental prediction.
originalProblem: Autonomous driving systems require enhanced robustness and improved understanding of complex, dynamic environments to ensure reliable operation.

A clear, unified understanding and detailed categorization of 'world models' in the context of autonomous driving, particularly their synergy with video generation, was lacking in existing literature.
Existing surveys on autonomous driving broadly covered component systems but lacked focused analysis on the specific interactions and structural parallels between generative models and world models.
results: Established a categorization of autonomous driving world models into Perception-Prediction structures (diffusion-based and autoregressive) and Reinforcement Learning structures.
Identified concrete examples of models leveraging structural parallels, such as DriveDreamer and OccSora for diffusion-based systems, and GAIA-1 and DriveGPT4 for autoregressive approaches in AD.

Delineated key challenges, including data scarcity, computational demands, privacy concerns, and the need for structural innovation, alongside future prospects like multimodal integration and diversified scene generation.
solution: Conducted a systematic review and synthesis of current literature on video generation and world models, specifically within autonomous driving.

Proposed a two-tiered categorization for autonomous driving world models: a Perception-Prediction Structure (including Diffusion-Based and Autoregressive-Based models) and a Reinforcement Learning Structure.
Analyzed the structural commonalities between diffusion-based video generation models and AD world models to identify synergistic benefits and architectural parallels.
paper_summary.summary: The survey by Ao Fu et al. examines the interplay of video generation and world models in autonomous driving, offering a structured categorization of AD world models and highlighting the significant architectural parallels, especially in diffusion-based systems. It identifies how these commonalities enhance AD system capabilities and details critical challenges and future research opportunities.

title: Exploring the Interplay Between Video Generation and World Models in Autonomous Driving: A Survey

abstract: The growing capabilities of AI in generating video content have brought forward significant challenges in effectively evaluating these videos. Unlike static images or text, video content involves complex spatial and temporal dynamics which may require a more comprehensive and systematic evaluation of its contents in aspects like video presentation quality, semantic information delivery, alignment with human intentions, and the virtual-reality consistency with our physical world. This survey identifies the emerging field of AI-Generated Video Evaluation (AIGVE), highlighting the importance of assessing how well AI-generated videos align with human perception and meet specific instructions. We provide a structured analysis of existing methodologies that could be potentially used to evaluate AI-generated videos. By outlining the strengths and gaps in current approaches, we advocate for the development of more robust and nuanced evaluation frameworks that can handle the complexities of video content, which include not only the conventional metric-based evaluations, but also the current human-involved evaluations, and the future model-centered evaluations. This survey aims to establish a foundational knowledge base for both researchers from academia and practitioners from the industry, facilitating the future advancement of evaluation methods for AI-generated video content.

keyInsights: Effective evaluation of AI-generated videos necessitates a dual focus: both adherence to human perception (quality, physical realism) and precise alignment with human textual instructions.
Benchmark datasets for AIGVE are evolving from simple video-opinion pairs to more complex video-instruction-opinion triplets to adequately capture the nuances of instructional alignment.

Evaluation methods are progressing towards integrated modeling approaches, increasingly leveraging Large Language Models (LLMs) for their reasoning capabilities and potential to provide more interpretable, holistic assessments.
originalProblem: A lack of a unified framework and systematic approach for evaluating the rapidly emerging field of AI-generated video content, leading to fragmented research.

Traditional Video Quality Assessment (VQA) frameworks are insufficient as they primarily focus on technical aspects and perceptual factors in user-generated videos, failing to address alignment with specific creator instructions or physical plausibility in AI-generated content.
The rapid development of new video generation models has outpaced the development of robust and comprehensive evaluation metrics, leaving a significant gap compared to AI-generated text and images.
results: The survey successfully formalizes AIGVE, providing a foundational knowledge base and a guiding framework for future research and development in this complex domain.

Current AI-generated video models, despite advancements, still exhibit deficiencies in consistently producing content that perfectly aligns with human perception (e.g., physical laws) and accurately fulfills complex human instructions.

Identified critical future research directions include integrating advanced Vision Language Models (VLMs), improving the interpretability of evaluation scores, and embedding ethical and safety considerations into AIGVE frameworks.
solution: Formally proposing "AI-Generated Video Evaluation (AIGVE)" as an emerging research field and establishing two principal criteria: alignment with human perception and alignment with human instructions.

Providing a systematic and comprehensive review of existing evaluation methodologies, including benchmark datasets and evaluation metrics drawn from traditional VQA, multimodal text-visual alignment, and nascent AIGVE methods.

Categorizing evaluation approaches into 'Metric Collection Evaluation' and 'Modeling Evaluation' (further split into general and specific-aspect models, and evolving from multimodal to LLM-based methods), offering a structured overview.
paper_summary.summary: University of California, Davis researchers present a comprehensive survey formally establishing AI-Generated Video Evaluation (AIGVE) as a distinct field. It details existing methodologies for assessing video quality and alignment with human instructions, categorizing approaches from metric collection to advanced modeling, and highlighting current limitations in generative AI video capabilities.

title: A Survey of AI-Generated Video Evaluation

abstract:
The rapid development of diffusion models has significantly advanced
AI-generated content (AIGC), particularly in Text-to-Image (T2I) and
Text-to-Video (T2V) generation. Text-based video editing, leveraging these
generative capabilities, has emerged as a promising field, enabling precise
modifications to videos based on text prompts. Despite the proliferation of
innovative video editing models, there is a conspicuous lack of comprehensive
evaluation benchmarks that holistically assess these models' performance across
various dimensions. Existing evaluations are limited and inconsistent,
typically summarizing overall performance with a single score, which obscures
models' effectiveness on individual editing tasks. To address this gap, we
propose EditBoard, the first comprehensive evaluation benchmark for text-based
video editing models. EditBoard encompasses nine automatic metrics across four
dimensions, evaluating models on four task categories and introducing three new
metrics to assess fidelity. This task-oriented benchmark facilitates objective
evaluation by detailing model performance and providing insights into each
model's strengths and weaknesses. By open-sourcing EditBoard, we aim to
standardize evaluation and advance the development of robust video editing
models.

organization_info.name: The Chinese University of Hong Kong, Shenzhen

title: EditBoard: Towards a Comprehensive Evaluation Benchmark for Text-Based  Video Editing Models

abstract: Text-to-video (T2V) generation technology holds potential to transform multiple domains such as education, marketing, entertainment, and assistive technologies for individuals with visual or reading comprehension challenges, by creating coherent visual content from natural language prompts. From its inception, the field has advanced from adversarial models to diffusion-based models, yielding higher-fidelity, temporally consistent outputs. Yet challenges persist, such as alignment, long-range coherence, and computational efficiency. Addressing this evolving landscape, we present a comprehensive survey of text-to-video generative models, tracing their development from early GANs and VAEs to hybrid Diffusion-Transformer (DiT) architectures, detailing how these models work, what limitations they addressed in their predecessors, and why shifts toward new architectural paradigms were necessary to overcome challenges in quality, coherence, and control. We provide a systematic account of the datasets, which the surveyed text-to-video models were trained and evaluated on, and, to support reproducibility and assess the accessibility of training such models, we detail their training configurations, including their hardware specifications, GPU counts, batch sizes, learning rates, optimizers, epochs, and other key hyperparameters. Further, we outline the evaluation metrics commonly used for evaluating such models and present their performance across standard benchmarks, while also discussing the limitations of these metrics and the emerging shift toward more holistic, perception-aligned evaluation strategies. Finally, drawing from our analysis, we outline the current open challenges and propose a few promising future directions, laying out a perspective for future researchers to explore and build upon in advancing T2V research and applications.
